metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State the stuck-state alarm threshold in the Design Context or S5c AC (e.g. 'alert if a change remains in the same phase for > 20 minutes') so the criterion is evaluable in-session.
findings.finding: The story-level AC bullet for S5c states that failure modes must be 'visible via metric/alarm', and names a 'stuck-state time-in-phase alarm', but neither the story nor the Design Context specifies the time-in-phase threshold that triggers the alarm — leaving the executor to choose an arbitrary value.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — S5c bullet; Design Context — 'stuck-state alarm' reference
findings.suggested_fix: Add to Design Context: "Stuck-state threshold: a change that has not advanced phase within 20 minutes triggers the `autolander_stuck_phase` alarm (configurable via env var `STUCK_PHASE_TIMEOUT_S`, default 1200)."

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Remove `:ro` from the autoheal Docker socket mount (S5a docker-compose.yml); autoheal requires write access to the socket to issue `docker restart` — the mount must be the default read-write bind.
findings.finding: S5a specifies the Docker socket is mounted 'read-only' for the autoheal sidecar, but autoheal issues `docker restart` (a daemon write operation); a :ro socket mount makes the restart action fail silently, negating the core autoheal reliability mechanism.
findings.location: S5a AC: 'the autolander service carries the autoheal=true label so the sidecar manages it'; S5a description: 'the Docker socket mounted read-only'
findings.suggested_fix: In docker-compose.yml, change the autoheal socket volume entry from `/var/run/docker.sock:/var/run/docker.sock:ro` to `/var/run/docker.sock:/var/run/docker.sock` (no `:ro`). If blast-radius is a concern, note that autoheal only calls restart on containers it labels — it does not have broad daemon control.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Correct the Docker socket mount for willfarrell/autoheal from `:ro` to read-write (no `:ro` suffix) in the S5a docker-compose.yml AC, and verify against willfarrell/autoheal's documentation that write access is required for restart operations.
findings.finding: The plan specifies the Docker socket for willfarrell/autoheal is mounted `:ro` (read-only), but autoheal's core function — restarting an unhealthy container — requires issuing `POST /containers/{id}/restart` to the Docker daemon, which is a write operation. A read-only socket mount will silently prevent autoheal from restarting the autolander container, defeating the stated reliability posture.
findings.location: S5a (08e1-5505-d898-4192) AC: 'Autoheal sidecar wired' — 'Docker socket mounted read-only'
findings.suggested_fix: Change the socket mount in docker-compose.yml from `- /var/run/docker.sock:/var/run/docker.sock:ro` to `- /var/run/docker.sock:/var/run/docker.sock` (no `:ro`), document the write-access rationale in a compose comment, and note the security trade-off (host socket access) in the design context.

findings.finding: The stuck-state alarm in S5c fires 'when a `wipChain` sits in any non-`idle` phase past a threshold' but the threshold value is never stated. Unlike the heartbeat contract (which explicitly states ≤15 s freshness and >90 s lander_down), the time-in-phase threshold has no specified default, no reference to a related timeout (e.g., the S2 30-min Verified-await bound), and no unit-check anchor.
findings.location: S5c AC: 'Stuck-state alarm (high visibility)'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a concrete verification mechanism for 'NEVER writes session_log' — e.g., a unit test asserting the autolander logger's handlers contain no session_log sink, OR a CI grep/lint rule that fails if `session_log` is imported/referenced in `infra/autolander/`.
findings.finding: The AC 'The service NEVER writes session_log' is a non-deferred, story-level requirement, but no verification mechanism is specified anywhere in the plan (no test assertion, no CI lint/grep rule, no log-output check). Without a falsifiable proof, this goal is unobservable at acceptance time.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — final bullet: 'The service NEVER writes session_log; all failure modes … are visible via metric/alarm for troubleshooting.'
findings.suggested_fix: In S5c or S5d, add a test (e.g., `test_no_session_log_handler`) that instantiates the loop service and asserts `session_log` is not among its log handlers; optionally add a `grep -r session_log infra/autolander/ && exit 1` CI step so the prohibition is enforced statically.

findings.finding: The title's headline deliverable — 'service logs, not session_log' — lacks a concrete, end-state-observable proof in the plan body. No log-routing config (compose log-driver, stdout redirect) is specified, no test asserts the absence of session_log writes, and no CI/smoke check is called out. This means the team cannot objectively verify the headline guarantee at deploy time.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — final bullet: 'The service NEVER writes session_log'
findings.suggested_fix: In S5c or S5d, add an AC line such as: 'A CI lint/grep step asserts no `session_log` import or call exists in `infra/autolander/`; the compose service definition sets `logging.driver: json-file` (or the site standard) to route container stdout/stderr to service logs.' Alternatively, add a unit test that monkeypatches `session_log` and asserts it is never called during a loop iteration.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Change the autoheal Docker socket mount from `:ro` to read-write (omit the `:ro` suffix, or use `:rw`) in `docker-compose.yml` — `autoheal` must issue `POST /containers/{id}/restart` which requires a writable socket; `:ro` silently disables all restarts.
findings.finding: The plan mounts the Docker socket for the `willfarrell/autoheal` sidecar as `:ro` (read-only), but autoheal must issue `POST /containers/{id}/restart` against the Docker daemon API — a write operation. A read-only Unix socket mount silently prevents all write syscalls, so the sidecar starts but never restarts an unhealthy container. The restart behaviour is then un-exercised by S5d's hang test (which asserts autoheal works), making the reliability guarantee non-functional at runtime.
findings.location: S5a AC: 'Autoheal sidecar wired: the `willfarrell/autoheal` service is present in `docker-compose.yml` with the Docker socket mounted **read-only**'
findings.suggested_fix: In docker-compose.yml, change `- /var/run/docker.sock:/var/run/docker.sock:ro` to `- /var/run/docker.sock:/var/run/docker.sock` (or `:rw`). The `:ro` annotation was likely added to limit socket exposure, but autoheal's operative function — restarting containers — requires write access to the Docker daemon API.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Change the autoheal Docker socket mount in docker-compose.yml from `:ro` to read-write (omit `:ro`), or document a concrete mechanism by which autoheal can restart containers through a read-only socket.
findings.finding: S5a mandates the willfarrell/autoheal Docker socket be mounted read-only (`:ro`), but autoheal requires write access to the Docker socket to issue container restart commands — mounting it `:ro` will silently break autoheal's ability to restart the autolander container.
findings.location: child 08e1-5505-d898-4192, AC 'Autoheal sidecar wired'
findings.suggested_fix: Remove the `:ro` qualifier from the Docker socket mount for the autoheal sidecar service in docker-compose.yml. The willfarrell/autoheal image requires write access (`/var/run/docker.sock:/var/run/docker.sock`) to call the Docker API restart endpoint.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Before committing the `project.config` `Rebase` stanza, probe the live Gerrit 3.14.1 `access-control.html` (or `/config/access/capabilities`) to confirm the exact access-right name and required form for `rebase_on_behalf_of_uploader` — add the verified stanza to S5b's AC (don't leave it as 'verify at implementation').
findings.finding: The Gerrit `Rebase` access right (needed for `rebase_on_behalf_of_uploader=true`) is a new integration surface on this specific Gerrit 3.14.1 instance that has never been used before. The plan explicitly defers verification to implementation ('Verify at implementation the exact access-right name/form for on-behalf-of-uploader rebase against this instance's `access-control.html`'). No probe of the live instance's `access-control.html` was done, and the access-right name, the exact `project.config` stanza, and whether a separate per-change-owner permission form is required are all unconfirmed. Neither `project.config` nor `setup-project.sh` currently contain the `Rebase` right.
findings.location: S5b AC: 'Rebase-on-behalf access right (corrected mechanism): … **Verify at implementation** the exact access-right name/form for on-behalf-of-uploader rebase against this instance's `access-control.html`'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit requirement in S5c's AC (and the monitoring_autolander.tf spec) that all three new CloudWatch alarms wire `alarm_actions` (and `ok_actions`) to the shared `aws_sns_topic.alerts.arn`, consistent with every other alarm in the repo.
findings.finding: The new CloudWatch alarms in monitoring_autolander.tf (NEW) are never specified to wire alarm_actions to the shared aws_sns_topic.alerts, so they could be created as silent alarms — directly contradicting the story's 'LOUD failures' and 'highly visible' reliability posture.
findings.location: S5c AC: 'Error metric (LOUD by default)' / 'Alarm in monitoring_autolander.tf' / S5 parent plan: 'fail LOUDLY'
findings.suggested_fix: In S5c's AC for the error-metric alarm and the stuck-state alarm, add: 'alarm_actions and ok_actions reference aws_sns_topic.alerts.arn (the shared alert sink declared in monitoring.tf), consistent with the repo alarm convention.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Declare the status endpoint's explicit access class in S5c (e.g., 'internal-only, bound to loopback/127.0.0.1 or Docker-network-internal, no auth required because non-secret and network-isolated' OR 'internal LAN, unauthenticated, rationale: non-PII operational metadata on a private network'); document the docker-compose port binding scope and any TLS intent.
findings.finding: The read-only HTTP status endpoint introduced in S5c is a network-reachable surface (`land`'s `lander_down` reads it remotely; the AC says 'bound so agents can reach it') but its access posture is never explicitly declared — the plan says 'Read-only, non-secret' but does not state whether it is public-with-rationale, internal-network-only, or loopback-restricted. Under the rubric, an undeclared access posture on a boundary-crossing path is itself the finding, regardless of whether the data is sensitive.
findings.location: S5c AC — 'Read-only status endpoint': 'bound so agents can reach it'; S5c child ticket description
findings.suggested_fix: In S5c's AC for the status endpoint, add: 'The endpoint MUST bind to 127.0.0.1 (or the Docker-internal network only) and NOT be published on a host port reachable from outside the Gerrit box, so no auth or TLS is required; if cross-host access is needed (e.g., land running on a different host), document TLS-or-SSH-tunnel requirement.' This makes the access posture explicit and auditable.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `stop_grace_period` to the `autolander` compose service (S5a) set to at least the bot's maximum expected drain duration (e.g., the CI-wait timeout plus a small buffer), so Docker does not SIGKILL the container before the drain and recovery.json write can complete.
findings.finding: S5d specifies the drain is 'time-bounded (SIGTERM deadline ~ the CI-wait timeout)', but docker compose uses a default stop_grace_period of 10 seconds before sending SIGKILL; if a CI-wait is in progress at shutdown, the bot is SIGKILLed before the drain completes and recovery.json is written, defeating the crash-safe recovery guarantee.
findings.location: S5d AC: 'Drain-on-shutdown + crash-safe recovery'; S5d description: 'the drain is time-bounded (SIGTERM deadline ~ the CI-wait timeout)'
findings.suggested_fix: In docker-compose.yml under the `autolander` service, add `stop_grace_period: <duration>` where duration ≥ the CI-wait timeout (e.g., `stop_grace_period: 600s` if CI is bounded at ~10 min). The drain itself should still enforce its own deadline and exit voluntarily before the grace period expires.

findings.finding: The read-only HTTP status endpoint (S5c) is a new network-reachable service whose human/admin authentication contract is not specified. The plan says 'bound so agents can reach it' and 'Read-only, non-secret' but never states the binding address (loopback vs. all-interfaces), whether/how it is proxied through the host nginx (the existing nginx config has no location block for it), or what human/admin auth mechanism (OIDC/HTTP-auth/gateway) applies. Without an explicit 'loopback-only' or 'behind the existing TLS+nginx gateway' declaration — and a corresponding nginx proxy rule — this endpoint's access posture is ambiguous; if it binds on 0.0.0.0 it is directly internet-reachable with no auth.
findings.location: AC: Observability + status endpoint (S5c) / S5c child ticket: 'Read-only, non-secret; bound so agents can reach it'
findings.suggested_fix: Bind the endpoint to `127.0.0.1:<AUTOLANDER_STATUS_PORT>` in docker-compose.yml (matching the review-bot pattern), add a `location /autolander/` proxy block in rebar.conf.template, and document that the endpoint is only reachable via the existing nginx TLS gateway — which enforces the same network access control as Gerrit and the review-bot. Then no additional auth layer is needed, and the rationale ('behind the existing TLS nginx gateway, single-tenant VPC') should be written into the plan as the explicit justification.

findings.finding: The read-only HTTP status endpoint (S5c) does not declare its access posture — whether it is public/unauthenticated, internal-network-only, or authenticated — making its trust boundary undeclared. "Bound so agents can reach it" is ambiguous about binding address and network reachability.
findings.location: S5c AC – 'Read-only status endpoint' / child ticket 9a6f-065d-91c4-4e03 AC line 1
findings.suggested_fix: Add a single line to S5c's AC: 'The endpoint MUST bind to 127.0.0.1 (or the Docker internal network) only; it is not published on the host interface and is not reachable from outside the Gerrit box.' If cross-host reachability is required (e.g., for a monitoring agent on another host), state that explicitly and confirm the VPC security group restricts access.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the concurrency model for the in-process HTTP status endpoint in loop.py (e.g., stdlib `http.server` in a daemon thread with a threading.Lock on wipChain state, or a separate subprocess), and either justify the departure from the established separate-service prior art (review-bot pattern) or adopt that pattern.
findings.finding: The plan embeds a read-only HTTP status endpoint directly inside `loop.py` (the synchronous 15s-poll loop process), but never specifies the threading/concurrency model for running the HTTP server concurrently with the blocking poll loop. This is a novel pattern in this codebase — the established prior art (review-bot) is a dedicated FastAPI/uvicorn async service process, not an HTTP server embedded in a polling loop. The plan cites Zuul `zuul-web` and Tide `deck` as prior art, but both are separate processes/services, not in-process servers embedded in the scheduling loop.
findings.location: S5c (9a6f-065d-91c4-4e03) AC: 'Read-only status endpoint' and Files: 'infra/autolander/loop.py — EDIT: the read-only HTTP status endpoint'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the complete canonical `phase` enum (e.g., `idle | rebasing | awaiting_verified | submitting | paused`) in the Design context section of S5 (or a shared schema doc referenced by all children), and annotate which values are 'non-idle' for the stuck-state alarm filter.
findings.finding: The `phase` enum value vocabulary is referenced but never canonically defined in S5 or any child. S5c's stuck-state alarm must filter 'non-`idle` phase'; S5d's crash-recovery decision table is keyed by phase value; S5c's status endpoint serves `phase` as a JSON field — yet the complete set of valid values (`idle`, `rebasing`, `awaiting_verified`, `submitting`, `paused`, possibly others) is only partially reconstructible by scavenging across child tickets, with no single authoritative definition anywhere in this plan or the linked repo.
findings.location: Design context; S5c AC 'Stuck-state alarm'; S5d AC 'Drain-on-shutdown + crash-safe recovery'; S5d AC 'Emergency-stop'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the S5 Design context: 'Phase enum (design-fixed): `idle` (no wipChain), `rebasing`, `awaiting_verified`, `submitting`, `paused` (emergency-stop active). Alarm fires on any non-`idle`, non-`paused` phase past the threshold — `paused` is intentional and must NOT alarm.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the exact stdout format for `AUTOLANDER_ERROR` and `AUTOLANDER_HANDBACK` markers (e.g., 'a JSON line containing the key `"marker": "AUTOLANDER_ERROR"`' or 'a line prefixed with the literal string `AUTOLANDER_ERROR`') co-located in S5c's AC so both loop.py and observability.sh implement the same contract.
findings.finding: The `AUTOLANDER_ERROR` and `AUTOLANDER_HANDBACK` structured stdout markers are referenced as the grep targets for two of three `autolander_errors` feeders in `observability.sh`, but their exact string format is never defined in S5 or any child. The existing observability pattern (confirmed in observability.sh lines 125, 148) uses `grep -cE 'VOTER_ERROR'` / `grep -cE 'MERGE_CHANGE_ERROR'` against journald output. S5c calls these 'structured stdout markers' but never specifies whether the grep key is a bare string, a JSON field, a prefix, or something else — leaving the feeder implementer to invent the format.
findings.location: S5c AC: 'Error metric (LOUD by default)' — feeders (1) and (2); S5c Files: `infra/scripts/observability.sh`
findings.suggested_fix: Follow the existing VOTER_ERROR / MERGE_CHANGE_ERROR pattern: define that the loop writes a line containing the literal token `AUTOLANDER_ERROR` (or `AUTOLANDER_HANDBACK`) to stdout, and that observability.sh greps for that exact token using `grep -cE 'AUTOLANDER_ERROR'`. A one-line format spec eliminates the contract gap.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify in S5d's crash-recovery decision rules whether `re_drive_count` from `recovery.json` is inherited, incremented, or reset at each recovery phase, and confirm whether the N=5 cap from S2b applies to crash-recovery re-drives.
findings.finding: The `recovery.json` schema includes a `re_drive_count` field (carried from S2b's bounded N=5 re-drive), but S5d's crash-recovery decision protocol never specifies whether this counter is incremented during a crash-recovery re-drive, whether the N=5 cap applies to post-restart re-drives, or whether the counter is reset on successful recovery. This is a counter with ambiguous placement across the schema-definition site (S5d) and the decision-rule site (also S5d, but silent on the counter).
findings.location: S5d AC: 'Drain-on-shutdown + crash-safe recovery' — recovery.json field list and `phase=submitting` / `mid-handback` decision rules

findings.finding: The `AUTOLANDER_ERROR` structured stdout marker (feeder 1 of the autolander_errors metric in S5c) is referenced by name but its JSON shape and the grep/parse pattern that `observability.sh` would use to count it are never defined. The plan cites `VOTER_ERROR`/`G2P` as prior art, but those patterns differ: `VOTER_ERROR` is a structured JSON keyword greppable by `grep -cE 'VOTER_ERROR'` against journald output. The exact field set of `AUTOLANDER_ERROR` (error type, change_id, stack_id, phase, etc.) is absent, yet `observability.sh` must be edited to count it.
findings.location: S5c AC: 'Error metric (LOUD by default)' — first feeder; child task description 'the AUTOLANDER_ERROR structured stdout marker'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a marker spec to S5c: 'The loop emits `AUTOLANDER_ERROR {"change_id":"...","phase":"...","error":"..."}` to stdout; `observability.sh` counts it via `grep -cE AUTOLANDER_ERROR` against the autolander container journal, matching the VOTER_ERROR pattern in section 4.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Name the autolander state volume explicitly in the S5a AC (e.g., `autolander_state`) and specify how `compose-up.sh` provisions it — either by extending `SITE_SUBDIRS` with a new entry + adapting `volume_for_subdir()` to handle non-`gerrit_` prefixes, or by provisioning it as a separate `docker volume create` call outside the `SITE_SUBDIRS` loop, ensuring the config-check.sh drift gate passes.
findings.finding: The plan instructs adding the autolander state volume to `SITE_SUBDIRS` in `compose-up.sh`, but the existing `SITE_SUBDIRS` naming convention is tightly coupled to Gerrit's stateful subdirectory layout: every entry is a bare subdir name (e.g., `git`, `logs`) that gets the `gerrit_` prefix via `volume_for_subdir()`. The autolander state volume is not a Gerrit subdir, so naming it as a bare word in `SITE_SUBDIRS` would either require extending the `volume_for_subdir()` function or accepting a mis-prefixed volume name (e.g., `gerrit_autolander-state`). The plan never names the autolander volume or explains how it fits this convention, leaving an implementer to guess whether to extend the function or use a different provisioning path.
findings.location: S5a AC: 'the autolander's named volume is declared `external: true` (the only volume pattern in this stack) and added to `SITE_SUBDIRS` in `compose-up.sh`'

findings.finding: Sibling tickets touch the same file(s) with no ordering edge.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a depends_on/blocks edge to serialize, or partition the file ownership.

findings.finding: Acceptance-criteria / definition-of-done quality issues.
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the status endpoint's binding address and port (e.g., loopback-only `127.0.0.1:<port>` proxied through the existing nginx), AND state how human/admin principals authenticate to it (e.g., behind the nginx TLS+GitHub-OAuth gateway, or explicitly justified as loopback-only / single-tenant private access).
findings.finding: The read-only HTTP status endpoint (S5c / loop.py) is never given a binding address, port, or human/admin authentication contract. The AC says only 'bound so agents can reach it'; the host lives in a public-subnet VPC with an internet-facing security group, so if the endpoint binds to 0.0.0.0 on any port, it is potentially internet-reachable with no stated auth.
findings.location: S5c AC: 'Read-only status endpoint' / S5c description: 'bound so agents can reach it'
findings.suggested_fix: Bind the endpoint to 127.0.0.1 on a dedicated port (e.g. 9090) and add an nginx location block that proxies it under the existing TLS+GitHub-OAuth-authenticated vhost (matching the pattern already used for Gerrit at :8080). Document this in S5c's AC as the binding + access contract.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a `phase=paused` case to S5d's crash-recovery decision table specifying the restart behaviour when the bot is killed while paused (e.g., 're-enter paused state immediately; sentinel file on the state volume determines this, no wipChain to reconcile').
findings.finding: S5d's crash-recovery decision table specifies rules for `phase=rebasing`, `phase=awaiting_verified`, `phase=submitting`, and `mid-handback` — but the same ticket introduces `paused` as a valid phase value via the emergency-stop feature. If the bot is killed while paused (e.g., operator stops it mid-emergency-pause for a redeploy), the recovery protocol has no decision rule for `phase=paused`, leaving an unspecified fallback.
findings.location: S5d AC: 'Drain-on-shutdown + crash-safe recovery' (decision table) and S5d AC: 'Emergency-stop'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the decision table: '`phase=paused` — sentinel file is still present on the state volume; re-enter `paused` on restart (no wipChain reconciliation needed, since `paused` means no work was in flight). The sentinel file IS the recovery record for this phase.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Update S5b's title to accurately reflect the scope: e.g., 'S5b Credentials (SSM) + rebar-project Rebase ACL' — removing 'All-Projects capability' and 'submit ACL provisioning'.
findings.finding: S5b's ticket title reads 'S5b Credentials (SSM) + All-Projects capability + submit ACL provisioning', but the AC body explicitly states the Rebase right is in the rebar project's own refs/meta/config — NOT All-Projects — and that no submit ACL is added. The title is a stale/misleading residual that contradicts the body.
findings.location: child ea61-01c3-daea-4824, ticket title vs. AC body
findings.suggested_fix: Rename ticket title to: 'S5b Credentials (SSM) + Rebase access right (rebar project refs/heads/*)' to match the AC body.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Document in S5a why `willfarrell/autoheal` is preferred over Docker's native `restart: unless-stopped` + HEALTHCHECK retry mechanism (which already restarts unhealthy containers), or replace the autoheal sidecar with the native pattern used by the existing services.
findings.finding: The plan introduces `willfarrell/autoheal` as a new third-party sidecar dependency without considering the established alternative already present in the codebase: Docker's native `restart: unless-stopped` policy (used by both existing services in docker-compose.yml) combined with the HEALTHCHECK directive. Docker's built-in restart policy already restarts a container that exits or fails its HEALTHCHECK after a configurable number of retries (`--health-retries`). The plan never explains why a third-party sidecar is needed over this established pattern, nor documents what autoheal provides beyond the native restart policy.
findings.location: S5a (08e1-5505-d898-4192) AC: 'Autoheal sidecar wired' and Design context: 'autoheal restart'
findings.suggested_fix: Add `restart: unless-stopped` to the autolander service (matching existing services) and rely on Docker's built-in unhealthy-container restart behavior (`--health-retries` failures trigger a restart under `unless-stopped`). Remove the autoheal sidecar to eliminate the Docker socket security exposure. Document this choice in the compose file comments.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Guard the `docker inspect --format '{{.State.Health.Status}}'` feeder in `observability.sh` against the `<no value>` / empty case (no HEALTHCHECK configured), and document in the S5c AC that this feeder only becomes active after S5d's HEALTHCHECK lands.
findings.finding: The `docker inspect --format '{{.State.Health.Status}}'` feeder for `autolander_errors` in `observability.sh` (S5c) is specified before the container `HEALTHCHECK` that populates `State.Health.Status` is added (that is S5d's deliverable). When S5c lands before S5d, Docker returns `<no value>` for the health status field (no HEALTHCHECK configured), so the feeder produces an empty/malformed value rather than `unhealthy`, silently breaking the third error feeder from the moment S5c ships until S5d lands.
findings.location: S5c AC: 'Error metric … (3) the container `unhealthy` Docker health-state (via `docker inspect --format '{{.State.Health.Status}}'`)'
findings.suggested_fix: In `observability.sh`, after capturing the health status, add: `[ "$health_status" = "unhealthy" ] && unhealthy_count=1 || unhealthy_count=0` — and treat any non-`unhealthy` value (including `<no value>`) as 0, not as an error.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define an explicit integration protocol for loop.py edits shared by S5c and S5d: either designate one child as owner of the complete loop.py structure and the other as a strict additive patch, or add a formal dep S5d→S5c (or vice versa) with an integration test that exercises both layers together.
findings.finding: `infra/autolander/loop.py` is claimed for substantive, interlocking edits by both S5c (status endpoint, error markers, metric emit) and S5d (heartbeat write, sentinel check, SIGTERM drain, crash-recovery) with no merge protocol, integration test, or ordering constraint between the two children.
findings.location: child 9a6f-065d-91c4-4e03 (Files: loop.py) and child 38be-a778-43f5-4a4e (Files: loop.py)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a formal ticket dependency S5d → S5a (S5d depends on S5a) to enforce Dockerfile.autolander exists before S5d's HEALTHCHECK edit is applied.
findings.finding: `infra/compose/Dockerfile.autolander` is created by S5a and then edited by S5d to add HEALTHCHECK, but no formal ticket dependency (S5d→S5a) is declared, creating an ordering gap that risks S5d being worked before the file exists.
findings.location: child 38be-a778-43f5-4a4e (Files: Dockerfile.autolander) and child 08e1-5505-d898-4192 (Files: Dockerfile.autolander)
findings.suggested_fix: Set S5d's `deps` to include `08e1-5505-d898-4192` (S5a) in the ticket metadata.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define `SITE_SUBDIRS` in the S5a AC or Design Context: state what it represents (e.g. space-separated host paths to bind-mount as state volumes), its expected format, and a concrete example value.
findings.finding: The `SITE_SUBDIRS` parameter referenced parenthetically in the S5a AC line is not defined anywhere in the plan — an executor would not know its expected values, format, or where it is used without stopping to ask.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — S5a bullet: '(+ compose-up.sh SITE_SUBDIRS)'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a sentence such as: "`SITE_SUBDIRS` — space-separated list of host-side state-volume root paths (e.g. `/srv/autolander/state`); the script bind-mounts each into the container at the same path."

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add to the plan body (or S5c child detail) an enumeration of the three `autolander_errors` feeders (e.g., rebase failure, submit failure, sentinel/stuck-state trigger) so the AC is fully grounded.
findings.finding: S5c AC references 'autolander_errors (three feeders + alarm)' but the plan body never describes what the three error feeders are, leaving the AC partially ungrounded — an implementer reading only the plan cannot know what to instrument.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — S5c bullet
findings.suggested_fix: In the Design context or S5c description, add a bulleted list: (1) rebase/merge error path in loop.py, (2) submit/push error path, (3) stuck-state/time-in-phase threshold breach — adjust to actual intended sources.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify in the plan (or in both child stories) that S5a owns the docker-autoheal container + label config and S5d owns the HEALTHCHECK directive that activates it, with an explicit note that S5d depends on the label convention established by S5a.
findings.finding: The autoheal mechanism is split across two children in a way that risks ownership confusion: S5a claims 'autoheal sidecar' (the docker-autoheal container in compose) while S5d claims 'HEALTHCHECK + autoheal' (the healthcheck that triggers it). Neither AC explicitly states the dependency relationship between these two halves.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — S5a bullet and S5d bullet
findings.suggested_fix: Add one sentence under the S5d AC bullet: 'The HEALTHCHECK directive must use the container label convention defined by the autoheal sidecar in S5a (e.g., `autoheal=true`).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Expose the three heartbeat thresholds (poll interval, freshness window, `lander_down` age) as named constants or env vars (e.g. `AUTOLANDER_POLL_S`, `AUTOLANDER_HEARTBEAT_STALE_S`, `AUTOLANDER_LANDER_DOWN_S`) so they can be tuned without code changes and are co-located as a single source of truth.
findings.finding: The heartbeat/liveness thresholds (15 s poll interval, ≤15 s freshness, >90 s `lander_down`) are described as 'design-fixed' with no mention of an env-var or config knob, yet they are operationally coupled: if the poll interval ever changes, the staleness thresholds must change in lockstep across the container, the status endpoint, and the `land` CLI. Hardcoding them makes that coordinated change error-prone.
findings.location: Design context — Heartbeat contract (design-fixed)
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the S5a/S5d compose definition: `AUTOLANDER_POLL_S=15`, `AUTOLANDER_HEARTBEAT_STALE_S=15`, `AUTOLANDER_LANDER_DOWN_S=90` as environment variables read by `loop.py` and the status endpoint; reference the same vars in `land`'s config.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a deliverable to capture the key architectural decisions (heartbeat-file-in-volume vs. DB, flock guard rationale, autoheal-over-k8s, deferred HA) in an ADR or a `docs/autolander-architecture.md` / `CLAUDE.md` entry referenced from the story.
findings.finding: The plan introduces a novel architectural pattern — heartbeat timestamp file in a named state volume, single-writer flock guard, autoheal sidecar, drain-on-SIGTERM recovery record, and the `heartbeat_age_s` status-endpoint contract — none of which are captured in an ADR or CLAUDE.md update. Future maintainers have no durable record of *why* these choices were made over alternatives (e.g., a DB-backed heartbeat, Kubernetes liveness probes, or an HA setup).
findings.location: Design context + Scope sections; AC S5a, S5c, S5d

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define a staged-rollout path for the initial production deployment: e.g., a `AUTOLANDER_PROJECT_ALLOWLIST` env var (or dry-run mode) that restricts the bot to a single pilot project/branch on first launch, with a documented step to widen scope after a validation period.
findings.finding: The plan deploys a net-new auto-submitting bot to production in one step with no feature flag, kill switch beyond the emergency-stop sentinel, or canary/staged-rollout mechanism to limit the blast radius. An auto-submit bot that fires on the wrong set of changes (misconfigured filter, stale S3 outcome marker, credential issue) would affect real changes immediately with no scoped rollout path.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — S5a (compose-up.sh); S5d (emergency-stop sentinel)
findings.suggested_fix: Add `AUTOLANDER_DRY_RUN=true` and/or `AUTOLANDER_PROJECT_ALLOWLIST=<pilot-project>` env vars to the compose definition; make the loop skip the actual submit (log only) when `DRY_RUN=true`, and document the promotion path in the story.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the deploy ordering constraint (S3 marker must be live before S5 is started) and define the graceful-degradation behavior in S5d if the outcome marker is absent (e.g., treat as 'no pending outcome → safe to drain').
findings.finding: The plan states S5 depends on S3's outcome marker for 'drain/restart reconciliation' but does not specify the deploy order or old+new coexistence contract: if S5 (the service) is deployed before S3's outcome marker is live in Gerrit, the drain logic that reads the outcome marker will either fail silently or behave incorrectly during the gap.
findings.location: Dependencies — 'Uses S3's outcome marker for drain/restart reconciliation'; AC S5d

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] S5c must specify (and test) the invocation mechanism for observability.sh (e.g., cron entry, systemd timer, compose service) so all three autolander_errors feeders are guaranteed to run.
findings.finding: The `observability.sh` host-script feeders for `autolander_errors` (including the `docker inspect` unhealthy-state feeder) have no specified invocation mechanism (cron, systemd timer, sidecar, etc.) — no child owns how or when this script is called.
findings.location: child 9a6f-065d-91c4-4e03, AC 'Error metric (LOUD by default)' — feeder #3 via docker inspect

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] S5d must explicitly list darksome-starry-sable (0525-6d71-8969-4512) as a formal ticket dependency and include a precondition check (or integration test step) that the S3 marker bucket is accessible from the autolander container with appropriate IAM permissions before the crash-recovery test runs.
findings.finding: S5d writes to 'S3's marker' for crash-safe recovery (drain outcome, acknowledged flag, recovery reconciliation), but no child provisions or validates that the S3 bucket/marker infrastructure exists and is accessible from the autolander container — its existence is assumed via a cross-story dependency on S3 (darksome-starry-sable).
findings.location: child 38be-a778-43f5-4a4e, AC 'Drain-on-shutdown + crash-safe recovery'

findings.finding: The third feeder of the `autolander_errors` metric in S5c is 'each `lander_down`/handback event.' The `lander_down` concept originates in S4 (the `land` command reads `heartbeat_age_s` from the HTTP status endpoint). `observability.sh` is a host-side bash script that has no access to S4's client logic. No protocol is defined for how `observability.sh` detects or counts a `lander_down`/handback event — no stdout marker, no log pattern, no state file, and no alternative detection mechanism.
findings.location: S5c AC: 'Error metric (LOUD by default)' — third feeder

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
